summary.intent: Extract the useful send_document feature work from a messy closed PR (PR #353) that was polluted with unrelated merge commits, and create a clean PR with just that feature.
summary.outcome: Successfully cherry-picked the send_document commit (eec08a2), manually resolved a sandbox.rs conflict by adapting to the refactored sandbox module structure, verified all tests pass, and committed the clean feature to a new branch ready for PR.

repo: PR #353 was closed and contained 33 commits, but only the last one (eec08a2) was the actual feature work — the rest were unrelated merges from main polluting the diff
The sandbox module was refactored from a single sandbox.rs file into a directory (sandbox/mod.rs, sandbox/containers.rs, sandbox/paths.rs) at some point after the original PR branch diverged

detected_container_cli() already existed in a more sophisticated form in sandbox/paths.rs taking a config parameter; the patch's simpler OnceLock version was added as a separate public container_cli() function
is_cli_available was already pub(crate) in the refactored sandbox module — one of the patch's changes was already present

The send_document feature includes: blocked extensions, MIME validation, size limits, shared file_io module (DRY with send_image), MIME maps in moltis_media::mime, filename field on MediaAttachment, Content-Disposition header on media API, document card UI component, and E2E tests

code.finding: Added public container_cli() function using OnceLock for podman/docker detection, plus podman localhost/ prefix stripping in list_sandbox_images()
Added container_cli to re-exports so crate::sandbox::container_cli() works from image_cache.rs and swift-bridge
Contains the more sophisticated detected_container_cli() that takes a config — distinct from the new simpler container_cli()

workflow: When a PR is polluted with merge commits, use git show <last-commit-sha> or git format-patch to extract just the relevant commit as a patch rather than cherry-picking the whole branch
git apply --3way --reject is useful for partial patch application — it applies what it can and creates .rej files for conflicts rather than rolling back everything
After applying a patch with rejects, check .rej files to understand what needs manual resolution rather than trying to re-apply the full patch

friction: sandbox.rs patch failed because the module had been refactored into a directory structure since the original branch diverged — required manual analysis and adaptation
git apply --3way rolled back entirely on conflict rather than applying partial changes, requiring a second pass with --reject flag
Multiple Read tool calls to the same cached tool-result file suggest the diff output was large and had to be read in chunks
open_items: PR not yet pushed/created in the transcript — commit was made but gh pr create step was not shown as completed
The document card UI component and CSS changes were applied via patch but not explicitly verified in the build output
E2E tests from the original commit were included but only unit tests were explicitly run to verify
